steps.title: Identify the core grammatical relationship
steps.content:
The sentence contains two independent clauses that must be joined:
Clause 1: “The sand at Tangsi Beach in Indonesia is a more unusual shade”
Clause 2: “deposits of crushed coral and other organic matter lend the sand a unique pink hue”
The word “though” serves as a transition between them. These clauses describe related but distinct ideas—the unusual nature of the sand paired with the explanation for it.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule for transitional junctures
steps.content: When a transition word like “though” bridges two independent clauses, punctuation both before and after the transition marks the boundary. A comma before “though” separates it from the first clause; a semicolon after “though” creates a clear break before the second clause begins, signaling the shift from the main observation to the explanation. This comma-though-semicolon pattern correctly articulates the relationship: the sand is unusual, and here is why. The other three options either fail to mark both boundaries (lacking the semicolon after “though”) or misuse punctuation by treating “though” as a parenthetical element rather than a transitional connector. That matches choice A — A is correct.
